content:
# Judges Chapter 3 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.9 — "deliverer" vs. "rescuer"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a savior for the sons of Yisrael" | moshia l'vnei Yisrael |
| LOGOS | "a deliverer for the people of Israel" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "a rescuer to save them" | "Rescuer" for moshia |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. moshia comes from the root ישע (yasha', "to save/deliver"). "Deliverer" captures both the military and theological dimensions. "Rescuer" (PNEUMA) reduces the term to a single event; "savior" (EMET) is technically accurate but imports NT connotations into an OT military context. "Deliverer" is the best balance.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.10 — "The Spirit of the LORD was upon him" vs. "The Spirit of the LORD came upon him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Spirit of YHWH was upon him" | vattehi alav ruach YHWH |
| LOGOS | "The Spirit of the LORD was upon him" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "The Spirit of the LORD came upon him" | "Came upon" for "was upon" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew says vattehi alav — "was upon him" (hayah + al). PNEUMA's "came upon" implies a moment of arrival, while "was upon" describes a state. The distinction matters: the Spirit's presence is described as a condition (being upon), not an event (coming upon). Other formulations in Judges use different verbs for the Spirit's dramatic onset (e.g., tsalach in 14:6, "rushed upon").

**Status:** Minor. State vs. event.

---

### v.15 — "a left-handed man" vs. "a man restricted in his right hand"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a man restricted in his right hand" | ish itter yad-y'mino |
| LOGOS | "a left-handed man" | Standard interpretation |
| PNEUMA | "a left-handed man" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. While EMET's literal rendering preserves the Hebrew idiom, "a man restricted in his right hand" is misleading in English — it suggests a disability rather than left-handedness. The Hebrew phrase itter yad-y'mino is a standard idiom for left-handedness (cf. Judges 20:16, where 700 Benjaminites are described with the same term). The narrative logic confirms it: Ehud uses his left hand (v.21), which is why the sword on his right thigh goes undetected.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET noted for literal value.

---

### v.22 — "the dagger came out behind" vs. "the king's bowels emptied"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it came out the back" | vayyetse haparshedonah |
| LOGOS | "the dagger came out behind" | parshedonah as anal exit |
| PNEUMA | "the king's bowels emptied" | Interprets parshedonah as excretion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS with note. parshedonah is a hapax legomenon — it occurs only here. Scholarly opinion is divided: (a) "it [the blade] came out the back/rear" (the dagger exited posteriorly), (b) "excrement came out" (the bowels voided at death), (c) "he went out to the porch" (Ehud exited). LOGOS's "the dagger came out behind" follows the majority scholarly reading while preserving ambiguity. PNEUMA's "bowels emptied" is one valid interpretation but presented as certain when the Hebrew is not.

**Status:** Minor but textually notable. Hapax legomenon — all translations are interpretive.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA changes state to event | 1 | "Came upon" for "was upon" (v.10) |
| PNEUMA selects one interpretation of ambiguity | 1 | "Bowels emptied" for hapax parshedonah (v.22) |
| Minor lexical choices | 2 | "Rescuer" for moshia (v.9), left-handedness idiom (v.15) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 3's disagreements are relatively minor. The most interesting is v.22 — the hapax parshedonah. When the Hebrew is genuinely uncertain, LOGOS preserves the ambiguity while PNEUMA picks one interpretation. The narrative itself trades in bodily humor and grotesque detail; each translation handles this differently.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
